title: Count primes up to N
description: Given N, return count of primes <= N

examples.explanation: Initialize count = 0. Check i=2: is_prime(2) returns True (no divisors from 2 to √2≈1, so prime). count=1. i=3: is_prime(3) returns True (no divisors from 2 to √3≈1). count=2. i=4: is_prime(4) returns False (divisible by 2). count remains 2. i=5: is_prime(5) returns True. count=3. i=6: is_prime(6) returns False (divisible by 2). i=7: is_prime(7) returns True. count=4. i=8: is_prime(8) returns False (divisible by 2). i=9: is_prime(9) returns False (divisible by 3). i=10: is_prime(10) returns False (divisible by 2). Final count = 4.

Initialize count = 0. Check numbers 2-20: 2 (prime, count=1), 3 (prime, count=2), 4 (not prime), 5 (prime, count=3), 6 (not prime), 7 (prime, count=4), 8 (not prime), 9 (not prime), 10 (not prime), 11 (prime, count=5), 12 (not prime), 13 (prime, count=6), 14 (not prime), 15 (not prime), 16 (not prime), 17 (prime, count=7), 18 (not prime), 19 (prime, count=8), 20 (not prime). Final count = 8.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n√n)
approaches.summary: Iterate through numbers from 2 to N, and for each number, check divisibility by all integers up to its square root to determine if it's prime. This is straightforward but inefficient for large N.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Primes Using Brute Force O(n√n)
ladders.desc: Complete implementation counting all prime numbers up to N using the brute force approach.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete solution to the problem using the brute force approach. The intuition is straightforward: we need to check every number from 2 to N to determine if it's prime, and count those that are. A number is prime if it's greater than 1 and has no divisors other than 1 and itself. The key optimization is that we only need to check divisibility up to the square root of the number, since any factor larger than the square root would have a corresponding factor smaller than the square root. The algorithm works by initializing a count to 0, then iterating i from 2 to N. For each i, we check if it's prime by testing divisibility by numbers from 2 to √i. If no divisor is found, we increment the count. The time complexity is O(n√n) because for each of n numbers, we perform up to √n divisibility checks. Space complexity is O(1) as we only use a few variables. This connects directly to L1's is_prime function, which is called for each number. Common mistakes include checking divisibility up to i instead of √i (inefficient), forgetting that 1 is not prime, and not handling edge cases like N < 2. The real code pattern involves a loop from 2 to N, calling is_prime on each number, and accumulating the count.

examples.trace: Initialize count = 0. Check i=2: is_prime(2) returns True (no divisors from 2 to √2≈1, so prime). count=1. i=3: is_prime(3) returns True (no divisors from 2 to √3≈1). count=2. i=4: is_prime(4) returns False (divisible by 2). count remains 2. i=5: is_prime(5) returns True. count=3. i=6: is_prime(6) returns False (divisible by 2). i=7: is_prime(7) returns True. count=4. i=8: is_prime(8) returns False (divisible by 2). i=9: is_prime(9) returns False (divisible by 3). i=10: is_prime(10) returns False (divisible by 2). Final count = 4.

Initialize count = 0. Check numbers 2-20: 2 (prime, count=1), 3 (prime, count=2), 4 (not prime), 5 (prime, count=3), 6 (not prime), 7 (prime, count=4), 8 (not prime), 9 (not prime), 10 (not prime), 11 (prime, count=5), 12 (not prime), 13 (prime, count=6), 14 (not prime), 15 (not prime), 16 (not prime), 17 (prime, count=7), 18 (not prime), 19 (prime, count=8), 20 (not prime). Final count = 8.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — is_prime Function
ladders.desc: Implement a function to check if a single number is prime using divisibility up to its square root.
ladders.explanation: This is the most important helper function for the brute force approach. The intuition is that a prime number has exactly two distinct positive divisors: 1 and itself. To check if a number n is prime, we test whether it's divisible by any integer from 2 up to √n. If we find any divisor, n is composite; otherwise, it's prime. We only need to check up to √n because if n has a divisor d greater than √n, then the corresponding divisor n/d would be less than √n, and we would have already found it. The algorithm: if n < 2, return False. For i from 2 to √n (inclusive), if n % i == 0, return False. If the loop completes without finding a divisor, return True. Time complexity is O(√n) for checking one number. Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into L0 by being called for each number from 2 to N. Common mistakes include checking up to n-1 (inefficient), not handling n < 2 correctly, and using floating-point math for √n which can cause precision issues (better to use integer comparison i*i <= n). The real code pattern uses a loop with condition i*i <= n.

examples.trace: n=7 ≥ 2. Check i=2: 7%2=1 ≠0. i=3: 3*3=9 >7, so stop. No divisors found, return True.
n=12 ≥ 2. Check i=2: 12%2=0, so divisible. Return False immediately.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Prime Checking via Divisor Search
ladders.desc: Determine if a number is prime by searching for divisors within a limited range.
ladders.explanation: This is the central technique: checking for divisors to determine primality. The intuition is that if a number n is composite, it must have at least one divisor d such that 2 ≤ d ≤ √n. This is because if n = a*b, then at least one of a or b must be ≤ √n (otherwise a*b > n). Therefore, we can limit our search to numbers up to √n, making the check efficient. The step-by-step logic: start with candidate divisor i=2. While i*i ≤ n, check if n % i == 0. If yes, n is composite. If not, increment i and continue. If the loop finishes without finding a divisor, n is prime. The time complexity for this check is O(√n). Space is O(1). This is the core of L1's is_prime function. Common mistakes include not using the square root optimization (checking up to n-1 gives O(n) time), or incorrectly handling edge cases like n=2 (where √2≈1.4, but we still need to check, though the loop condition i*i ≤ 2 means i=2 gives 4>2, so it skips and correctly returns prime). The real code pattern uses a while loop with i*i <= n.

examples.trace: i=2: 2*2=4 ≤9, 9%2=1 ≠0. i=3: 3*3=9 ≤9, 9%3=0, so divisor found. Return composite (False).
i=2: 4 ≤11, 11%2=1 ≠0. i=3: 9 ≤11, 11%3=2 ≠0. i=4: 16 >11, stop. No divisors found, return prime (True).

ladders.title: Building Block — Square Root Optimization
ladders.desc: Understand why checking divisibility only up to the square root is sufficient.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept explains the mathematical justification for limiting divisor checks to √n. The intuition is based on factor pairs: if n is composite, it can be written as n = a * b, where a and b are integers greater than 1. Without loss of generality, assume a ≤ b. Then a ≤ √n because if both a and b were greater than √n, their product would exceed n (since √n * √n = n). Therefore, at least one factor (the smaller one, a) must be ≤ √n. This means that to check if n has any divisors, we only need to test numbers from 2 to √n. If no divisor is found in this range, n cannot have any divisors greater than √n either (because they would come in pairs with a smaller divisor we would have already found). The algorithm implication: instead of looping i from 2 to n-1 (O(n)), loop while i*i ≤ n (O(√n)). This is a key optimization that makes brute force feasible for moderate n. Time analysis: reduces checks from linear to square root. Space is constant. This feeds into L2 by providing the range for divisor search. Common mistakes include using floating-point sqrt which can be imprecise for large n (better to use integer comparison i*i ≤ n), or misunderstanding the logic and checking up to √n exclusive (should be inclusive, as n could be a perfect square like 25 where divisor 5 equals √25). The real code pattern uses i*i <= n as the loop condition.

examples.trace: √16 = 4. Factor pairs: (2,8), (4,4). The smaller factors are 2 and 4, both ≤4. Checking up to 4 will find divisor 2 or 4.
√17 ≈ 4.12. If 17 were composite, it would have a factor ≤4.12. Checking 2,3,4 shows no divisors, so 17 is prime.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Modulo Division Check
ladders.desc: Perform the modulo operation to test if one number divides another.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation is checking divisibility using the modulo operator (%). The intuition is that if a number a divides number b, then b % a should equal 0, meaning there is no remainder when b is divided by a. In the context of prime checking, we use this to test if any integer i divides n. The operation is simple: compute n % i. If the result is 0, i is a divisor of n, so n is composite. If non-zero, i does not divide n evenly. The algorithm step: for each candidate divisor i, compute remainder = n % i. If remainder == 0, return False (not prime). Otherwise, continue. Time complexity per check is O(1). Space is O(1). This feeds into L3 as the core operation within the square root loop. Common mistakes include confusing modulo with integer division (using / instead of %), or not understanding that modulo works for positive integers (ensure n and i are positive). Also, in some languages, modulo for negative numbers might behave differently, but in our problem n ≥ 2. The real code pattern is a simple if statement: if n % i == 0: return False.

examples.trace: Compute 10 divided by 3: quotient 3, remainder 1. So 10 % 3 = 1 ≠ 0, so 3 does not divide 10.
Compute 10 divided by 5: quotient 2, remainder 0. So 10 % 5 = 0, so 5 divides 10.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Prime Number Definition
ladders.desc: Understand what a prime number is: a positive integer greater than 1 with no positive divisors other than 1 and itself.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: defining prime numbers. The intuition is that prime numbers are the building blocks of integers, as every integer greater than 1 can be factored uniquely into primes (Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic). A prime number is defined as a natural number greater than 1 that has exactly two distinct positive divisors: 1 and itself. For example, 2, 3, 5, 7 are primes. Numbers like 4, 6, 8 are composite because they have additional divisors (e.g., 4 is divisible by 2). The number 1 is not prime because it has only one divisor (1 itself). The algorithm to understand this: given a number n, list its positive divisors. If the only divisors are 1 and n, then n is prime. This is a conceptual check, not code. Time and space are not applicable here as it's a definition. This feeds into L4 because modulo checks are used to test for divisors other than 1 and n. Common mistakes include thinking 1 is prime (it's not), or including negative numbers (primes are defined for positive integers only). Also, some might forget that prime numbers must be greater than 1. The real code pattern starts with if n < 2: return False. This foundation is essential for all higher levels.

examples.trace: Divisors of 5: 1 and 5. Only two divisors, so 5 is prime.
Divisors of 6: 1, 2, 3, 6. More than two divisors, so 6 is composite.

approaches.name: Sieve of Eratosthenes O(n log log n)
approaches.summary: Use a boolean array to mark multiples of primes as non-prime, starting from 2, and count the remaining primes. This is efficient and commonly used for counting primes up to a limit.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Primes Using Sieve of Eratosthenes
ladders.desc: Implement complete solution to count all prime numbers up to N using Sieve of Eratosthenes algorithm.
ladders.explanation: The Sieve of Eratosthenes is an ancient algorithm for finding all prime numbers up to a given limit N. The intuition is simple yet powerful: instead of testing each number individually for primality (which would be O(N√N)), we systematically eliminate composite numbers by marking their multiples. We start with the smallest prime (2) and mark all its multiples as composite. Then we move to the next unmarked number (which must be prime) and repeat. This works because any composite number must have a prime factor ≤ √N, so we only need to sieve up to √N. The algorithm creates a boolean array of size N+1 where True means prime and False means composite. We initialize all entries as True (except 0 and 1), then iterate from 2 to √N. For each prime p, we mark all multiples starting from p² as False (since smaller multiples would have been marked by smaller primes). Finally, we count all True values. The time complexity is O(N log log N) due to harmonic series analysis of marking multiples. Space is O(N). This is optimal for counting all primes up to N when N is known in advance. Common mistakes include: 1) Sieving up to N instead of √N (inefficient), 2) Starting to mark multiples from p instead of p² (redundant work), 3) Not handling edge cases like N < 2, 4) Using 1-indexing incorrectly. The algorithm connects to the full problem by providing the complete counting mechanism.

examples.trace: Initialize: is_prime = [F, F, T, T, T, T, T, T, T, T, T] (indices 0-10). Sieve up to √10 ≈ 3. p=2: mark multiples from 4: 4→F, 6→F, 8→F, 10→F. p=3: mark multiples from 9: 9→F. Count primes: indices 2,3,5,7 are True → 4 primes.

Initialize: is_prime[0..20] all True except 0,1. Sieve up to √20 ≈ 4. p=2: mark 4,6,8,10,12,14,16,18,20 → F. p=3: mark 9,15 → F (6,12,18 already marked). p=4: not prime (already False). Count: 2,3,5,7,11,13,17,19 → 8 primes.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Sieve Marking Process
ladders.desc: Implement the core marking function that eliminates multiples of a given prime.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the Sieve algorithm. Given a prime number p and a boolean array is_prime representing numbers up to N, we mark all multiples of p (starting from p²) as composite (False). The intuition: if p is prime, then all numbers of the form k×p (where k≥p) are composite because they have p as a factor. We start from p² because smaller multiples (2p, 3p, ..., (p-1)p) would have been marked by smaller primes when we processed those primes earlier. For example, when p=5, we don't need to mark 10 (2×5) because it was marked when p=2, or 15 (3×5) because it was marked when p=3. This optimization reduces work significantly. The algorithm: for j from p² to N, increment by p, set is_prime[j] = False. Time complexity for marking multiples of one prime is O(N/p) operations. Space is O(1) additional. This connects to L0 by being the repeated operation in the main sieve loop. Common mistakes: 1) Starting from p instead of p² (inefficient), 2) Using wrong increment step, 3) Not checking bounds (p² might exceed N). The real pattern: for j in range(p*p, N+1, p): is_prime[j] = False.

examples.input: p=3, N=20, initial array all True
examples.output: Array with multiples of 3 marked False
examples.trace: Start: is_prime[0..20] all True. p=3, start from 9, mark 9→F. Increment by 3: 12→F, 15→F, 18→F. Stop at 21 > 20. Result: indices 9,12,15,18 are False, others unchanged.

examples.input: p=5, N=30, initial array all True
examples.output: Array with multiples of 5 marked False
examples.trace: Start: all True. p=5, start from 25, mark 25→F. Increment by 5: 30→F. Stop at 35 > 30. Result: indices 25,30 are False. Note: 10,15,20 were marked by smaller primes earlier.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Identifying Next Prime in Sieve
ladders.desc: Find the next unmarked number after current position in the boolean array.
ladders.explanation: This is the control logic that drives the Sieve algorithm forward. After marking multiples of the current prime, we need to find the next prime to process. The intuition: in the boolean array where True means potentially prime, the next True value after the current index must be prime because all its smaller factors would have been eliminated. For example, after processing p=2, the next True value is 3, which is prime. After p=3, the next True is 5, which is prime. This works because any composite number has a prime factor ≤ its square root, so if we've processed all primes up to √N, any remaining True number must be prime. The algorithm: starting from current index+1, scan the array until finding a True value or reaching √N. Time: O(√N) in worst case. Space: O(1). This connects to L1 by determining which prime to pass to the marking subroutine. Common mistakes: 1) Not limiting search to √N (unnecessary), 2) Off-by-one errors in indexing, 3) Assuming the next number is always prime without checking the array. Real pattern: for i in range(current+1, int(sqrt(N))+1): if is_prime[i]: return i.

examples.input: current=2, N=10, array after marking 2's multiples
examples.trace: Array: [F,F,T,T,F,T,F,T,F,T,F] (0-10). Start from 3, check is_prime[3]=True → found 3 immediately.

examples.input: current=3, N=20, array after marking 3's multiples
examples.trace: Array has F at multiples of 2 and 3. Start from 4: is_prime[4]=False. Check 5: True → return 5.

testCases.input: current=5, N=30, array T except composites
current=7, N=50, array T except composites
current=11, N=100, array T except composites
current=13, N=150, array T except composites
current=23, N=30, array T except composites

ladders.title: Building Block — Initialize Boolean Prime Array
ladders.desc: Create and initialize the boolean array for sieve algorithm.
ladders.explanation: This foundational step sets up the data structure for the Sieve. We need a boolean array of size N+1 (to include index N) where each index i represents whether number i is prime. The intuition: we start by assuming all numbers ≥2 are prime (True), then gradually mark composites as False. We explicitly set 0 and 1 as False because they're not prime by definition. The array provides O(1) access to check or update primality status. Time: O(N) to initialize. Space: O(N). This connects to L2 by providing the array to scan for next primes. Common mistakes: 1) Creating array of size N (missing index N), 2) Forgetting to set 0 and 1 as False, 3) Using wrong initial values. Real pattern: is_prime = [False, False] + [True]*(N-1).

examples.trace: Create array size 6 (0-5). Set index 0=False, 1=False. Set indices 2-5=True. Result: [F,F,T,T,T,T].

examples.output: [False, False, True, True, True, True, True, True, True, True, True]
examples.trace: Size 11. 0=F,1=F. 2-10=True → [F,F,T,T,T,T,T,T,T,T,T].

testCases.expected: [False, False, True, True, True, True, True, True]
[False, False, True, True, True, True, True, True, True, True, True]
[False, False, True, True, True, True, True, True, True, True, True, True, True, True, True, True]
[False, False, True, True, True, True, True, True, True, True, True, True, True, True, True, True, True, True, True, True, True]
Array length 31 with first two False, rest True

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Check and Mark Array Index
ladders.desc: Check if a number is marked as prime, and mark it as composite if needed.
ladders.explanation: This is the atomic operation in the Sieve algorithm. Given a boolean array and an index i, we need to: 1) Check if is_prime[i] is True (meaning i is currently considered prime), and 2) Optionally set it to False (mark as composite). The intuition: the array represents our current knowledge about primality. Initially all numbers ≥2 are True. As we discover that a number has factors, we flip it to False. This operation is O(1) time and space. It connects to L3 as the fundamental action performed on the initialized array. Common mistakes: 1) Accessing out-of-bounds indices, 2) Confusing True/False meanings, 3) Not checking before marking. Real pattern: if is_prime[i]: is_prime[i] = False.

examples.trace: Check is_prime[4]=True. Set is_prime[4]=False. Array becomes [F,F,T,T,F,T].
Check is_prime[6]=True. Set to False. Result: [F,F,T,T,F,T,F,T] (index 6 changed).

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Prime Numbers
ladders.desc: Learn what prime numbers are and how to recognize them.
ladders.explanation: A prime number is a natural number greater than 1 that has exactly two distinct positive divisors: 1 and itself. For example, 2, 3, 5, 7 are prime. Numbers like 4, 6, 8, 9 are composite (not prime) because they have additional divisors. The number 1 is not prime because it has only one divisor. The intuition: primes are the building blocks of all natural numbers through multiplication (Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic). To check if a number n is prime, we test divisibility by all integers from 2 to √n. If any divides n evenly, n is composite. This is O(√n) time. This foundation connects to L4 because the Sieve algorithm needs to identify primes to eliminate their multiples. Common misunderstandings: 1) Thinking 1 is prime, 2) Not understanding why we only check up to √n, 3) Confusing prime with odd (2 is prime but even). Real pattern: if n < 2: return False; for i in range(2, int(sqrt(n))+1): if n % i == 0: return False; return True.

examples.trace: 7 > 1. Check divisors: 2 (7%2=1), 3 (7%3=1), √7≈2.6 so stop at 2. No divisors found → prime.
9 > 1. Check divisors: 2 (9%2=1), 3 (9%3=0) → divisible by 3 → composite.

approaches.name: Sieve of Euler O(n)
approaches.summary: A linear sieve that builds a list of primes and marks each number only once using its smallest prime factor, ensuring linear time complexity. It's optimal for scenarios requiring prime factorization or minimal time.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Primes Using Euler's Linear Sieve
ladders.desc: Implement the complete Sieve of Euler algorithm to count all prime numbers up to N.
ladders.explanation: The Sieve of Euler is a linear-time algorithm that counts primes up to N by marking each composite number exactly once using its smallest prime factor. The intuition is that every composite number can be uniquely represented as the product of its smallest prime factor and another number, allowing us to process each number in constant time. We maintain two arrays: `is_prime` to track prime status and `primes` to store found primes. For each number `i` from 2 to N, if `is_prime[i]` is true, we add `i` to the primes list. Then, for each prime `p` in primes, we compute `composite = i * p`. If `p` divides `i`, we break to avoid redundant marking, ensuring each composite is marked only by its smallest prime factor. This break condition is key to linear complexity. The algorithm runs in O(n) time because each composite is processed once, and O(n) space for the arrays. Connection to L1: This full solution integrates the prime marking subroutine from L1 into the complete counting logic. Common mistakes include forgetting the break condition (which leads to O(n log log n) time like the classic sieve) or off-by-one errors in array bounds. Real code pattern: Initialize arrays, iterate through numbers, update primes list, mark composites with the break condition, and finally count primes by summing the boolean array or using the primes list length.

examples.trace: Initialize: is_prime = [True] * 11 (indices 0-10), primes = []. Process i=2: is_prime[2]=True, add 2 to primes. Mark composites: p=2, composite=2*2=4, mark is_prime[4]=False. i=3: is_prime[3]=True, add 3 to primes. Mark: p=2, composite=3*2=6, mark is_prime[6]=False; p=3, composite=3*3=9, mark is_prime[9]=False. i=4: is_prime[4]=False, skip adding to primes. Mark: p=2, composite=4*2=8, mark is_prime[8]=False; since p=2 divides i=4, break. i=5: is_prime[5]=True, add 5 to primes. Mark: p=2, composite=5*2=10, mark is_prime[10]=False; p=3, composite=15>10, stop. i=6 to 10: similar marking. Final primes <=10: 2,3,5,7 → count=4.

Initialize arrays for N=20. Process i=2: add prime 2, mark 4. i=3: add prime 3, mark 6,9. i=4: mark 8, break. i=5: add prime 5, mark 10,15,20. i=6: mark 12, break. i=7: add prime 7, mark 14 (since 7*2=14), 21>20 stop. i=8: mark 16, break. i=9: mark 18, break. i=10: mark 20 (already marked), break. i=11: add prime 11, mark 22>20 stop. i=12 to 20: continue marking with breaks. Primes found: 2,3,5,7,11,13,17,19 → count=8.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Mark Composites with Smallest Prime Factor
ladders.desc: Implement the core marking step of Euler's sieve that processes each number to mark its composites.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the heart of Euler's sieve, responsible for marking composite numbers in linear time. The intuition is that for each number i, we iterate through the list of primes found so far and mark i * p as composite, but we stop if p divides i. This ensures each composite is marked exactly once by its smallest prime factor. For example, when i=4 and p=2, we mark 8 and break because 2 divides 4; if we continued, we might mark 4*3=12, but 12's smallest prime factor is 2, so it should be marked when i=6 and p=2. The algorithm: Given i and the primes list, for each prime p in primes, compute composite = i * p. If composite exceeds N, break the loop. Mark composite as not prime. If p divides i, break to avoid redundant marking. Time complexity is amortized O(1) per number because each composite is marked once, leading to O(n) total. Space is O(n) for the boolean array. Connection to L0: This subroutine is called for each i in the full solution to build the prime count. Common mistakes include missing the divisibility break condition or incorrect loop bounds. Real code pattern: Loop through primes, compute composite, check bounds, mark false, and break if p divides i.

examples.trace: For i=6, primes list has [2,3,5]. p=2: composite=6*2=12, mark 12 as not prime. Check if 2 divides 6? Yes (6%2==0), so break loop. Do not process p=3 or p=5. Thus, only 12 is marked here; 18 will be marked when i=9 and p=2.

For i=10, primes=[2,3,5,7]. p=2: composite=10*2=20, mark 20 as not prime. Check divisibility: 2 divides 10? Yes, break. So only 20 is marked; 30 and 50 are marked elsewhere (e.g., 30 when i=15 and p=2, 50 when i=25 and p=2).

ladders.title: Core Logic — Break Condition for Linear Time
ladders.desc: Understand and implement the divisibility check that ensures each composite is marked once.
ladders.explanation: The core insight of Euler's sieve is the break condition: when processing a number i with a prime p, if p divides i, we break the loop to avoid marking composites multiple times. This works because every composite number has a unique smallest prime factor. By breaking when p divides i, we ensure that composite numbers are only marked when i is the product of their smallest prime factor and another number. For example, consider the composite 12. Its smallest prime factor is 2. It should be marked when i=6 and p=2 (since 6*2=12), not when i=4 and p=3 (4*3=12). When i=4, we have p=2, mark 8, and since 2 divides 4, we break, skipping p=3. This guarantees linear time complexity. The algorithm: For a given i and prime p, after marking i*p, check if i % p == 0. If true, break out of the prime iteration loop. Time analysis: This condition ensures each composite is processed once, so O(1) amortized per number. Space is negligible. Connection to L1: This logic is embedded in the marking subroutine to control the loop. Common mistakes include using continue instead of break or missing the condition entirely. Real code pattern: Inside the prime loop, after marking, add 'if i % p == 0: break'.

examples.trace: For i=4, with prime p=2. Compute composite=4*2=8, mark it. Check if 2 divides 4: 4 % 2 == 0, so break the loop. Do not process next prime p=3. This prevents marking 12 (4*3) here.
For i=6, prime p=2. Composite=6*2=12, mark it. Check divisibility: 6 % 2 == 0, so break. This ensures 18 (6*3) is not marked now; it will be marked when i=9 and p=2.

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterate and Maintain Prime List
ladders.desc: Implement a loop that iterates through numbers and dynamically builds a list of primes.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves iterating from 2 to N and maintaining a list of prime numbers found so far. The intuition is that as we process each number i, we check if it is prime by using a boolean array, and if so, we add it to the primes list. This list is then used in the marking step to generate composites. The algorithm: Initialize an empty list called primes. For i from 2 to N, check if is_prime[i] is true (initially all true except 0 and 1). If true, append i to primes. This step is simple but crucial because the primes list grows as we discover new primes, and it feeds into the marking logic. Time complexity is O(n) for the iteration, and space is O(n) for the list. Connection to L2: This iteration provides the i and primes list for the break condition logic. Common mistakes include starting the loop from 1 instead of 2 or incorrectly updating the boolean array. Real code pattern: Loop for i in range(2, N+1): if is_prime[i]: primes.append(i).

examples.trace: Initialize is_prime all true for 0-10. i=2: is_prime[2]=true, add 2 to primes. i=3: true, add 3. i=4: false (marked earlier), skip. i=5: true, add 5. i=6: false, skip. i=7: true, add 7. i=8,9,10: false, skip. Final primes list: [2,3,5,7].

Start with is_prime all true. As we iterate: i=2 add 2, i=3 add 3, i=4 skip, i=5 add 5, i=6 skip, i=7 add 7, i=8 skip, i=9 skip, i=10 skip, i=11 add 11, i=12 skip, i=13 add 13, i=14 skip, i=15 skip. Primes list: [2,3,5,7,11,13].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Check Divisibility and Mark Boolean Array
ladders.desc: Perform fundamental operations: check if a number divides another and update a boolean array.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on two basic operations: checking divisibility (i % p == 0) and marking an index in a boolean array as false. The intuition is that these are low-level steps used repeatedly in the sieve. Divisibility check determines if a prime p is a factor of i, which controls the break condition. Marking the boolean array updates the prime status of composite numbers. The algorithm: For divisibility, use the modulo operator; for marking, set is_prime[composite] = False. These operations are O(1) in time and space. Connection to L3: These operations are used within the iteration to build the prime list and mark composites. Common mistakes include index out-of-bounds errors when marking or incorrect modulo logic. Real code pattern: if i % p == 0: break; and is_prime[composite] = False.

examples.input: Check if 4 divides 12, mark index 12 in array
examples.trace: Divisibility: 12 % 4 == 0, so true. Marking: Assuming is_prime is a boolean array, set is_prime[12] = False to indicate 12 is not prime.

examples.input: Check if 3 divides 10, mark index 15 in array
examples.trace: Divisibility: 10 % 3 == 1, so false. Marking: Set is_prime[15] = False, as 15 is composite.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Prime Numbers and Boolean Arrays
ladders.desc: Understand what prime numbers are and how to use a boolean array to track them.
ladders.explanation: This foundation level introduces prime numbers and boolean arrays. A prime number is a natural number greater than 1 that has no positive divisors other than 1 and itself. For example, 2, 3, 5 are primes. A boolean array is a list of True/False values used to represent states; here, we use it to mark whether each number up to N is prime or not. Initially, we assume all numbers from 0 to N are prime (True), then we mark composites as False. The intuition is that by tracking primes in an array, we can efficiently count them later. The algorithm: Create an array is_prime of size N+1, set all entries to True, then set is_prime[0] and is_prime[1] to False since 0 and 1 are not primes. This setup is the starting point for any sieve algorithm. Time and space are O(n) for initialization. Connection to L4: This boolean array is used in the basic operations for marking and checking. Common mistakes include forgetting to set 0 and 1 as non-prime or using the wrong array size. Real code pattern: is_prime = [True] * (N+1); is_prime[0] = is_prime[1] = False.

examples.output: is_prime = [False, False, True, True, True, True]
examples.trace: Create array of size 6 (indices 0-5). Set all to True: [True, True, True, True, True, True]. Set is_prime[0]=False, is_prime[1]=False. Result: [False, False, True, True, True, True], meaning 2,3,4,5 are initially considered prime.

examples.output: is_prime = [False, False, True, True, True, True, True, True, True, True, True]
examples.trace: Array size 11. Initialize all True. Set index 0 and 1 to False. Result: [False, False, True, True, True, True, True, True, True, True, True] for numbers 0-10.

testCases.expected: [False, False, True, True, True, True, True]
[False, False, True, True, True, True, True, True]
[False, False, True, True, True, True, True, True, True]
[False, False, True, True, True, True, True, True, True, True]
